\begingroup
\fvset{listparameters={%
  \setlength{\topsep}{0pt}%

\begin{Verbatim}[breaklines=true,formatcom=\color{ada_blue}]
# Calculate the range of revenue and production
rev_range <- range(USSteamCoEstim$revenue, na.rm = TRUE)
prod_range <- range(USSteamCoEstim$production, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create the plot
time_series_plot <- ggplot(data = USSteamCoEstim, aes(x = date)) +
  # First time series (revenue)
  geom_line(aes(y = revenue), colour = "#00338D") +
  # Second time series (production), rescaled to match revenue range
  geom_line(aes(y = scales::rescale(production, to = rev_range)),
            colour = "#BC204B") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Revenue ($)",
    labels = scales::label_comma(),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(
      ~scales::rescale(., from = rev_range, to = prod_range),
      name = "Production (x 1000 lb)",
      labels = scales::label_comma()
    )
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.title.x = element_blank(),
    axis.title.y.left = element_text(color = "#00338D"),
    axis.title.y.right = element_text(color = "#BC204B")
  )
time_series_plot
\end{Verbatim}
\endgroup
\par\addvspace{\topsep}


\noindent Figure 5.1 shows a scatter plot of the independent variable production ($x$-axis) and the dependent variable revenue ($y$-axis) for the Case: US SteamCo. The plot is an easy means to get a feel for the relationship.
